output.lesson_title: Circle Theorems: Angle at the Centre
output.objective: Apply the circle theorem that the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.

segments.narration: Today we look at the first and most powerful circle theorem: the angle at the centre.
segments.subtitle: Angle at the Centre Theorem

visual.title: Circle Theorems
visual.subtitle: Angle at the Centre
visual.objective_text: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.

segments.narration: When two chords meet the circumference at point P, and both chords extend to points A and B on the circle, the angle at the centre is double the angle at the circumference.
segments.subtitle: Centre angle = 2 × circumference angle

visual.definition: The angle subtended by an arc at the centre of a circle is twice the angle subtended by the same arc at the circumference.

segments.narration: Draw a circle with centre O. Mark points A and B on the circumference, and P elsewhere on the circumference. Join OA, OB, PA, PB.
segments.subtitle: Construct the configuration

segments.narration: Split the figure along OP. Both triangles OPA and OPB are isosceles because OA, OB, and OP are all radii. Using the exterior angle of each triangle gives the result.
segments.subtitle: Proof via isosceles triangles

steps.reason: Radii of the same circle are equal
Base angles of an isosceles triangle
Base angles of an isosceles triangle

steps.statement: Exterior angle at O in OAP = 2a; at O in OBP = 2b
steps.reason: Exterior angle = sum of two interior opposite angles
Angle at centre is twice angle at circumference

segments.narration: A common mistake: applying the theorem when P is on the same side as the centre — here the reflex angle rule applies.
segments.subtitle: Watch the reflex case

mistaken_approach.statement: Assuming centre angle is always 2× circumference angle, regardless of P's position.
mistaken_approach.example: When P is on the minor arc, the centre angle is the reflex angle — still 2× the circumference angle.

correct_approach.statement: Identify which arc contains P; use the angle standing on the opposite arc at the centre.
correct_approach.example: If ∠APB = 140°, the centre angle on the same side as P is the reflex: 2 × 140° = 280°.

visual.why_students_make_it: They picture a single diagram and don't check the two possible arcs.

segments.narration: Recap: draw the configuration, check which arc, remember the factor of two, and use isosceles radii in proofs.
segments.subtitle: Check the arc — factor of 2 — radii

key_points: Centre angle is twice the circumference angle subtending the same arc.
Check which arc P sits on — reflex applies otherwise.
Radii are always equal, creating isosceles triangles.
Exterior-angle result is the key proof step.
visual.closing_statement: You can apply and prove the angle-at-the-centre theorem.

branch_templates.branch_narration_template: An arc subtends an angle when two chords from the arc's endpoints meet at a point — the angle at that meeting point is the subtended angle.
OAP is isosceles because OA and OP are both radii of the circle, so they are equal in length — two equal sides means two equal base angles.
